summary: DHH recounts his tenth participation in the 24 Hours of Le Mans during its centenary year. The race was marked by extreme drama — crashes, monsoon rain, mechanical failures, and a gravel trap incident that cost his team any chance at victory. Despite the disappointing result, DHH found satisfaction in his personal lap times, finishing as the fifth-fastest silver driver. He reflects on the cruel randomness of Le Mans, where hundreds of perfect laps can be undone by one split-second mistake, and wrestles with whether to continue racing given his recent four-year streak of bad luck after four podiums in his first six attempts.
key_points: The 2023 Le Mans centenary was exceptionally dramatic, with 22 cars retiring from crashes or mechanical issues throughout the 24-hour race
DHH's team went from leading the race to falling a lap behind through a sequence of tire debris, rain crashes, and a gravel trap incident
Driving without the pressure of winning can be liberating — DHH set his best lap times in the final stints when the result was already lost
DHH's last four Le Mans attempts have all been foiled by mechanical issues, contrasting sharply with four podiums (including a 2014 win) in his first six
Le Mans punishes premature optimism — the race is long enough that early leads mean nothing and one split-second mistake erases hundreds of clean laps
money_quotes: That's the danger of Le Mans. The race is so long that it's easy to start dreaming about results well before it's prudent.
Never do that at Le Mans.
You can do literally hundreds of laps across the French countryside with perfect poise, and then you make one tiny mistake, in a split second, and it all goes away.

It's a strange sensation, but in some ways it can be liberating to drive at Le Mans when you're no longer in it to win it. All the pressure is gone. It's just you and the track and the commitment to go flat out, damn the risks.
You never know whether you're going to get another shot at running at Le Mans.
Maybe Le Mans is trying to tell me that I've already had more than my fair share of luck at Circuit de la Sarthe.
When the pace is still solid, it's hard to commit to calling it quits.
key_insight: Le Mans is a metaphor for the cruelty of long games — hundreds of laps of perfect execution can be erased by one moment of error, and the hardest part is deciding when you've pushed your luck far enough.
